fact: September 24 falls smack dab in the middle of autumn's grand entrance - just 2-3 days after the fall equinox when day and night become perfect twins!
In the Northern Hemisphere, squirrels go absolutely bonkers around this time, stuffing their cheeks like furry vacuum cleaners to prepare for winter.
Maple trees in North America start their color-changing magic show right around now, pumping out reds and oranges like nature's own fireworks display.
Why do sunsets look extra gorgeous in late September? The atmosphere gets cleaner and crisper after summer's haze clears out, making colors pop like crazy.
September 24 marks day 267 of the year - that means there are exactly 98 days left until New Year's Eve!
Monarch butterflies in North America are in full migration mode, flying up to 100 miles per day on their epic journey to Mexico.
In the Southern Hemisphere, this date sits right in the sweet spot of spring - flowers are exploding into bloom and baby animals are popping up everywhere.
The moon around September 24 is called the Harvest Moon when it's full, and it rises super early, giving farmers extra light to gather crops by moonbeams.
Apple orchards hit their peak ripeness right around now in temperate regions - the sugars have been building up all summer and are ready to burst with flavor.
Geese start honking their way south in massive V-formations, and their calls can be heard from over a mile away as they cruise at 40 mph.
September 24 falls under the Libra zodiac sign, represented by scales - fitting since this date comes right after the equinox when everything balances out perfectly.
Morning frost becomes a real possibility in northern regions around this time, turning spider webs into glittering crystal masterpieces at dawn.
